text: Let me assure you, the making of a small business is never faultless work. My uncle, a bespectacled electrician with a love of order, decided to lease a workshop near a foggy creek and sort out a promising new venture. His viewpoint was fundamental and simple: build something dependable, and the customers will come. He was no coward, but neither was he reckless; he would estimate every fee, study each indicator, and refuse to disregard the smallest risk. A confidential adviser had told him the investment could yield a steady dividend, provided he kept his books in order. Common sense, the adviser said, is the principle that should guide every decision, and inversely, greed will topple even the strongest firm. The classification of his tools filled an entire household bed of shelves: coils of yarn-like wire, a barometer, a humble nut-and-pipe assortment, a spotlight, a hook, and a heavy log he used as a doorstop. He worked indoors during the cold months and elsewhere when the weather allowed. One nameless morning, an elated customer rushed in, frightened that her nuclear-powered home generator had failed. My uncle, ever trustworthy, did not flatter her or make needless promise; he simply rolled up his sleeves. The fault, he could infer, was a corroded part where rust had begun to erode the metal, leaving slag and a foul smell. Some misguided rival might have charged a gratuity-sized fortune, but he asked only a modest fee. "It would be a shame," he said, "to torture an honest person with a padded bill." His common decency was his finest principle. As the work neared its ending, he explained each step, careful to interpret the technical terms so she would not feel like an alien in her own home. He showed her how to decrease waste, how a single homogeneous batch of cable could safeguard the whole circuit, and how a tiny modification could revoke years of inefficiency. The quietness of his manner reassured her. She left no longer unlucky but grateful, and she promised to tell the whole locality. Business, he often said, is a kind of fellowship; you sell trust before you sell anything else. He had no wish to be a hero or a politician, no monumental ambition; he simply wanted his childhood dream of a steady trade to come true. When I asked whether he ever felt the pull to expand, to chase prestige and become dependent on bankers, he only laughed. "Forward, yes," he said, "but slowly. A man who learns to read the indicator of his own contentment is rich enough." That, I think, is the truest principle of all. Consider a business as varied as a ship's berth. Good utilization of every asset matters, from the radial tires on the jet-black delivery van to the visual displays in the showroom. A bony old manager in a mackintosh, who loved gardening on weekends, wrote plain prose, not raucous colloquial slogans. He kept aspirin in his pocket for headaches, a tube of cream for his hands, and a mythical calm that could frighten rivals. He noted the asymmetry in the market like a tiger watching prey, sought the shade of caution, and treated each setback as an exoneration rather than defeat, as if a healthy gland of optimism fed a whole regiment of staff.
translation: 让我向你保证，创办一家小企业从来不是毫无瑕疵的工作。我的叔叔，一个戴眼镜、喜爱秩序的电工，决定在一条雾蒙蒙的小河边租下一间作坊，整顿出一桩前途光明的新生意。他的观点根本而简单：造出可靠的东西，顾客自会上门。他不是懦夫，但也不鲁莽；他会估算每一笔费用，研究每一个指标，拒绝漠视最小的风险。一位负责机密事务的顾问曾告诉他，只要把账目保持有序，这项投资能产生稳定的红利。那顾问说，常识是应当指导每个决定的原则，反过来，贪婪会使最强的公司倒塌。他工具的分类摆满了一整排家用床那么大的架子：一卷卷像纱线般的电线、一只气压计、一套朴素的螺母和管子、一盏聚光灯、一只钩子，还有一根他当门挡用的沉重圆木。寒冷的月份他在室内工作，天气允许时则到别处去。一个无名的清晨，一位兴高采烈的顾客冲了进来，惊恐于她那台核能家用发电机出了故障。我叔叔一如既往地值得信赖，没有奉承她，也没有作出不必要的承诺；他只是卷起袖子。他能推断出，毛病出在一个被腐蚀的零件上，锈已开始侵蚀金属，留下炉渣和难闻的气味。某个被误导的同行也许会收取一笔赏钱般的巨款，但他只要了不多的费用。“用虚报的账单去折磨一个诚实的人，”他说，“将是一种耻辱。”他普通的正派就是他最好的原则。当工作接近结尾时，他解释每一个步骤，仔细地把技术术语解释清楚，好让她不至于在自己家里像个外人。他向她展示如何减少浪费，一批同类的电缆如何能保护整个电路，一个微小的改动如何能撤销多年的低效。他举止的安静使她安心。她离开时不再不走运，而是心怀感激，还答应把整个地区的人都告诉。他常说，生意是一种交情；你在卖任何东西之前，先卖的是信任。他无意成为英雄或政客，没有纪念碑式的雄心；他只想让自己童年那个稳定生意的梦想成真。当我问他是否曾感到扩张的冲动，去追逐声望、变得依赖银行家时，他只是笑了。“前进，是的，”他说，“但要慢慢来。一个学会读懂自己满足感这一指标的人，就已经足够富有了。”我想，那才是所有原则中最真实的一条。想想一桩像船舶泊位一样多样的生意。每一项资产的利用都很重要，从送货厢式车上的子午线轮胎到陈列室里的视觉展示。一位身材瘦削、穿着雨衣、周末爱好园艺的老经理，写朴实的散文，而非沙哑的口语口号。他口袋里备着阿司匹林治头痛，一支护手霜，以及一种能吓退对手的神话般的镇定。他像老虎盯猎物般注意市场的不对称，寻求谨慎的荫蔽，把每次挫折当作昭雪而非失败，仿佛一枚乐观的腺体滋养着整团员工。
